name: Maria Orsic
description: Maria Orsic (also spelled Oršić) is a figure from esoteric and conspiracy lore, primarily associated with claims of early German flying saucer development and occult societies in the early 20th century. According to these narratives, Orsic was a prominent medium, involved with the Vril Society and the Thule Society, who purportedly channeled extraterrestrial intelligences. These channeled messages allegedly provided advanced technological knowledge that enabled Nazi Germany to develop advanced aircraft, including flying saucers. The core of the story claims that she led a group of female mediums who communicated with alien races residing near Alpha Centauri or in other dimensions. These communications supposedly provided blueprints for advanced spacecraft, leading to secret projects aimed at building these craft. Mainstream historical and scientific communities dismiss these claims as pseudoscience and conspiracy theories lacking credible evidence. No verifiable historical documents exist to confirm Maria Orsic's existence or her involvement with any secret societies or technological projects. The narrative surrounding her is often attributed to post-war science fiction and alternative history authors who embellished existing occult and conspiracy theories related to Nazi Germany. The lack of primary source documentation, combined with the extraordinary nature of the claims, places Maria Orsic firmly within the realm of fringe theories.
subtitle: Alleged medium of Nazi UFO lore

claims.claim: Maria Orsic was a powerful medium.
claims.evidence: Accounts from purported members of the Vril and Thule Societies.
claims.criticism: No independent verification of her existence or mediumship abilities exists. Accounts are based on unsubstantiated rumors and post-war fiction.

claims.claim: She channeled extraterrestrial knowledge that led to advanced German aircraft.
claims.evidence: Claims of alien blueprints and technological specifications received through channeling.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence of such blueprints or technologies has ever been found. The narrative relies on speculation and lacks any scientific basis.

claims.claim: She was a leader in the Vril Society.
claims.evidence: Mention in some alternative history books and websites.
claims.criticism: The Vril Society itself is largely considered a fictional or highly embellished entity, with limited historical evidence to support its existence as described in conspiracy theories.

keyFigures.name: Jan van Helsing (Jan Udo Holey)
keyFigures.role: Author who popularized many of the Maria Orsic claims.

keyFigures.name: Norbert Jürgen-Ratthofer
keyFigures.role: Esoteric author who expanded upon Orsic's alleged role in UFO development.

keyFigures.name: Peter Bahn
keyFigures.role: Researcher of alternative history, critical of unverified claims surrounding Nazi occultism.

mythologyConnections: Germanic Neopaganism (potential influence on occult societies)

timeline.event: Alleged formation of the Vril and Thule Societies.
Purported German development of advanced aircraft based on channeled knowledge.
Emergence and popularization of the Maria Orsic narrative in alternative history literature.

sources.author: Jan van Helsing (Jan Udo Holey)

sources.title: Jan van Helsing (Jan Udo Holey)
